figures.gloss: The strictly unified, incorporeal Creator of the universe. Samaritans emphasize radical monotheism, referring to God as 'Shema' (The Name) to avoid pronouncing the Tetragrammaton, preserving the absolute sanctity and unapproachable nature of the divine presence.
figures.facet: Fiercely rejects any later Jewish mystical emanations, angelic intercessors, or hypostases (like the Rabbinic Shekhinah). God is utterly singular, accessible only through the unchanging Law and the ancient priesthood, requiring no prophetic mediation after Moses.
figures.provenance: Samaritan Pentateuch (Deuteronomy 6:4); Memar Marqah; Samaritan liturgy.

figures.gloss: The ultimate, unrepeatable conduit of divine revelation. To Samaritans, Moses is not merely the greatest of prophets, but the only true prophet ever sent by God to humanity, a being whose light pre-existed the world.
figures.facet: Unlike Rabbinic Judaism, Christianity, or Islam, which accept lines of subsequent prophets, Samaritanism dogmatically asserts that prophecy ended forever with Moses. Anyone claiming prophetic authority after him is inherently false.

figures.gloss: The sacred mountain rising above Shechem, ordained by God as the true, eternal dwelling place of the divine presence. In Samaritan tradition, it is the highest mountain on earth, the sole peak to survive the great flood, and the actual site of Eden.
figures.facet: The definitive theological rupture with Judaism. Samaritans claim the Jewish sanctuary at Jerusalem is a corrupted shrine established by Eli, a schismatic priest, while Gerizim remains the original, uncompromised geographic center of the covenant.
figures.provenance: Samaritan Pentateuch (Tenth Commandment in Exodus 20 and Deuteronomy 5); Book of Joshua (Samaritan version).

figures.gloss: The eschatological 'Restorer' or 'Returner' expected at the end of days. A human descendant of Joseph or Moses who will reunite the Israelites, recover the hidden tabernacle vessels on Mount Gerizim, and live a mortal life before dying.
figures.facet: The Taheb is strictly a restorer of the ancient order, not a divine savior or world-ending cosmic judge. He does not resurrect the dead universally or alter the Law; he simply ends the epoch of divine disfavor and restores proper Gerizim worship.
figures.provenance: Samaritan eschatology; Memar Marqah (Book 4); Abu l-Hasan al-Suri.

figures.gloss: The bipartite division of historical time. 'Rahuta' was the era of divine favor spanning from the conquest of Canaan to the schism of Eli. 'Fanuta' is the current dark age of divine hiding and displeasure, waiting to be broken by the Taheb.
figures.facet: Time is not merely fallen; the loss of grace is tied to a specific geographic and priestly schism. Redemption is not cosmic recreation, but a literal return to the pre-schism operational status of the Gerizim cult.

figures.gloss: The strict, unaltered Five Books of Moses, preserved in the Paleo-Hebrew script. It is the absolute, exhaustive blueprint of reality, containing all laws, history, and theology required for human life.
figures.facet: Radically exclusive. It explicitly commands the building of the altar on Mount Gerizim and entirely rejects the Prophets (Nevi'im), the Writings (Ketuvim), and the Rabbinic Oral Law (Talmud) as later Judahite fabrications.
figures.provenance: The Abisha Scroll; extant Samaritan Pentateuch manuscripts.

figures.gloss: The original holy implements of the wilderness Tabernacle, concealed in a cave on Mount Gerizim by the high priest Uzzi when the era of divine displeasure (Fanuta) began.
figures.facet: Unlike Jewish lore which places the hidden Ark under the Temple Mount in Jerusalem, Samaritan theology anchors the cosmos' missing operational core securely inside Mount Gerizim, physically awaiting the Taheb's arrival.
figures.provenance: Book of Asatir; Samaritan Chronicle II; Josephus ('Antiquities' 18.4.1).

figures.gloss: The annual slaughter and roasting of Passover lambs by the assembled Samaritan community on Mount Gerizim, performed by the priesthood exactly per the instructions of Exodus 12 — timed by the priestly calendar, the meat eaten standing, in haste, unbroken into the present day.
figures.facet: The only continuously practiced biblical animal sacrifice anywhere: not a revival or a reconstruction but an unbroken observance, making Gerizim's ritual counterpart to the mountain itself concrete rather than merely doctrinal — the sanctuary is not only claimed as holy, it is actively used exactly as commanded.
figures.provenance: Widely documented modern eyewitness and scholarly accounts of the annual Samaritan Passover on Mount Gerizim (near Nablus/Shechem); Exodus 12 is the community's own cited authority for its exact form.

figures.gloss: The hereditary Aaronic high-priestly line, claimed through descent from Phinehas, that leads the Samaritan community: fixing the calendar, keeping the Abisha Scroll, and officiating the Passover and the festival calendar at Gerizim.
figures.facet: The living institutional spine of a priest-led, not rabbi-led, religion: where Judaism's authority passed to rabbinic scholars after the Temple's fall, Samaritanism kept its authority in an unbroken priestly line — the community's own answer to the question of who may speak for the Law.
figures.provenance: Samaritan communal structure as documented from late antiquity to the present (Josephus; the Samaritan Chronicles; modern ethnographic and communal accounts of the Gerizim priesthood).

figures.gloss: 3rd-4th century CE Samaritan leader who reorganized communal liturgy, law, and structure during Roman rule — building synagogues, establishing schools, and consolidating the community's institutions during what the chronicles remember as its golden age.
figures.facet: The great historical reformer of every Samaritan chronicle: his era anchors the tradition's own memory of its cultural and demographic peak, functioning as a kind of second founding after Moses and Joshua rather than a mere administrator.
figures.provenance: Central figure of the Samaritan Chronicles (Tolidah; Continuatio of Abu'l Fath); dated to the Roman period on the strength of the chronicle tradition, though precise regnal-year dating varies across sources.

figures.gloss: 4th-century CE sage-poet whose Tibat Marqe / Memar Marqah and liturgical hymns form the foundation of Samaritan theology and its Aramaic liturgy; revered second only to Moses among human figures in the tradition.
figures.facet: The theologian who did the systematic work Moses' own text does not: since Samaritan dogma holds that prophecy ended with Moses, Marqe's towering status has to be built entirely on scholarship and poetry rather than any claim to revelation — the tradition's model of legitimate religious authority after the prophetic line is closed.
figures.provenance: Memar Marqah / Tibat Marqe manuscripts; cited repeatedly as provenance elsewhere in this roster (defter, torah, taheb entries) without previously having its own node.

figures.gloss: Moses' conqueror-successor, who in Samaritan tradition establishes the sanctuary at Gerizim and opens the era of divine favor (Rahuta); the Samaritan Book of Joshua is a distinct chronicle tradition, not a version of the Jewish text of the same name.
figures.facet: Holds a far higher place here than in Judaism: where Rabbinic tradition treats Joshua as a capable successor, Samaritanism makes him the one who inaugurates the entire golden age the community's own periodization (Rahuta) is measured from — successor as epoch-founder.
figures.provenance: The Samaritan Book of Joshua (Kitab Yashu), a distinct chronicle composition from the Hebrew Bible's Joshua; the Fanuta/Rahuta periodization ties his conquest to the start of the age of grace.

figures.gloss: The Samaritan common prayer book — the core liturgical corpus in Samaritan Aramaic, recited at synagogue services and festivals, whose oldest layers (hymns attributed to Marqe and to Amram Darah) date to the 4th century CE.
figures.facet: The point where Samaritan theology is actually enacted rather than merely asserted: the strict monotheism of Shema, the singularity of Moses, and the sanctity of Gerizim are doctrines the Pentateuch states, but it is the Defter's hymns that Samaritans actually speak aloud week to week.
figures.provenance: Samaritan liturgical manuscripts; Marqe and Amram Darah are named in Samaritan liturgical scholarship as the authors of its oldest hymn-layers.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The sacred mountain and the hidden ritual core buried within it.
Intra-tradition: The prophesied restorer who arrives specifically to end the age of Fanuta and return the world to Rahuta.
Intra-tradition: The sole legitimate prophet and the exhaustive, perfect text he transmitted.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The identical God of Israel, derived from the same ancient root, though Samaritanism preserves a strictly non-emanational theology compared to later Rabbinic Judaism.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both enforce a radical, indivisible monotheism that strictly rejects any partners, emanations, or divine incarnations.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the ultimate unknowable God, though Samaritanism fiercely rejects the Jewish Kabbalistic idea that God generates subsequent sephirotic emanations.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A singular, foundational prophet who dictates an exhaustive, unalterable system of law to a specific people before permanently departing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both claim the absolute status of the final, ultimate conduit of divine truth, foreclosing any future legitimate prophets.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Samaritan theology explicitly identifies Mount Gerizim as the unflooded summit of the Garden of Eden from the shared Genesis account.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The central cosmic mountain that serves as the unshakeable geographic and spiritual axis of the world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A specific, physical architectural space that serves as the uncorrupted structural blueprint for cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A future restorer who arrives to end a dark age, though the Taheb is strictly mortal and restores a specific cult rather than permanently recreating the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are eschatological figures tied to salvation, though the Taheb is a purely human restorer of the Law, explicitly lacking divine sonship.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Time divided into epochs of descending grace and eventual restoration, progressing inexorably based on cosmic law rather than divine whim.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An absolute, pre-existing divine blueprint that dictates both the structure of the cosmos and the precise ritual duties of humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A rigidly observed, closed corpus of revealed texts that governs all acceptable interactions with the divine.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A profoundly potent artifact whose concealment or loss plunges the world into a state of lack, waiting to be recovered.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve sealing away the seeds of a perfect order inside a hidden subterranean enclosure, to be opened when the age of darkness ends.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel: the primordial, ancient sage who alone possesses the definitive words and instructions for his people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The utterly singular, incorporeal source of all existence that cannot be divided or approached without strict mediation.

GOLD SEAM: Two refusals of the rabbinic chain — but Samaritans keep only their own Pentateuch while Karaites keep the full Tanakh; independent splits (medieval polemics linking Karaites to the Sadducees are contested, not descent).
Literal same-prophet identity: Samaritanism's 'Sole Prophet' is this same Exodus lawgiver, elevated in Samaritan doctrine above all subsequent revelation and prophecy.

the same Pentateuchal lawgiver read through the Israelite-Samaritan schism — Samaritanism holds Moses as its sole true prophet from the same Exodus-Sinai narrative, diverging over the Torah's text and Israel's sacred mountain.
The sacred mountain and its defining living observance: the sanctuary claimed as holy in doctrine is the sanctuary where the community actually sacrifices every year.
The priesthood's authority is inseparable from the mountain it serves — it fixes the calendar and officiates the rites that only occur there.
The high priest Uzzi, per Samaritan chronicle, is the one who concealed the Tabernacle vessels on Gerizim when Fanuta began — the priesthood's founding act of custodianship.
Baba Rabba's reorganization of communal liturgy and structure in the 3rd-4th century is the institutional counterpart to the Defter's own oldest liturgical layers, composed in the same period.
Marqe's hymns, alongside Amram Darah's, form the Defter's oldest and foundational layer — author and liturgy are directly bound.
Marqe is revered second only to Moses among human figures — the tradition's highest human authority standing directly beneath its sole prophet, never claiming to rival him.
Successor and predecessor: Joshua receives the conquest and the sanctuary from the one prophet Samaritanism recognizes, without himself gaining any prophetic status.
The era of divine favor (Rahuta) is dated by Samaritan tradition from Joshua's conquest of Canaan to the schism of Eli — his campaign opens the very age the periodization names.

At the umbrella-Abrahamic grain, Judaism, Christianity, and Islam collectively hold Moses among a continuing prophetic line; Samaritanism narrows the entire scripture and prophetic office to him alone — same historical figure, opposite exclusivity claim.

Same man, incompatible offices: Samaritanism's Sole Prophet of the one God versus hoodoo's paradigm conjurer whose biblical miracles are read as rootwork — prophet of law versus master of power, no descent between the two readings.

The Positivist Calendar's first month venerates Moses as a secular benefactor of Humanity, one name among thirteen; Samaritanism carries the same historical-legendary person as the Sole Prophet of God — worship with the revelation surgically removed.

dossier.essence: Samaritanism is the ancient Israelite religion of the Samaritans (who call themselves Shomrim, 'Keepers' of the Torah), today a community of only around 800 living at Kiryat Luza on Mount Gerizim and at Holon in Israel. They worship the one God of Israel, hold Moses to be the sole and final prophet and the Torah the only scripture, and insist that Mount Gerizim, not Jerusalem, is God's chosen sanctuary. They understand themselves as descendants of the northern Israelite tribes who kept the true cult, and regard Judaism as a later deviation.
dossier.cosmos: A radical, imageless monotheism: God is one, incorporeal, and creator, revealed through Moses and the Torah alone, with no later prophets, emanations, or mediators. History is split into the age of divine favor (Rahuta) and the present age of disfavor or 'turning away' (Fanuta), which began when the priest Eli illegitimately moved the cult away from Gerizim. Redemption will come with the Taheb, 'the Restorer' — a mortal figure of the line of Moses or Joseph who will restore Gerizim worship and recover the hidden Tabernacle vessels — followed by a day of judgment. Mount Gerizim is held to be the highest point on earth, the one peak unflooded, and the site of Eden.
dossier.practice: Religious life is governed strictly by the written Torah under a hereditary priesthood (the Cohen line, after the Aaronic high-priestly line ended in 1624): Sabbath, circumcision, purity laws, and the biblical festivals kept by the Samaritans' own reckoning. Its most striking surviving rite is the annual Passover sacrifice of lambs, still performed publicly on Mount Gerizim, the focus of pilgrimage. The community preserves its Torah in the Samaritan (Paleo-Hebrew–derived) script and its own liturgical tradition.
dossier.canon: The Samaritan Pentateuch, diverging from the Masoretic text in thousands of places (notably the Gerizim commandment) and preserved in manuscripts including the revered Abisha Scroll. Its own literature includes the Aramaic Targum, the theological 'Memar Marqah,' the 'Defter' liturgy, and chronicles such as the 'Tulida' and the 'Asatir.'
dossier.reflects: The survival of a rival Israelite orthodoxy — the ancient religion of Israel defined by the Torah alone, Moses alone, and one sacred mountain, set against the whole later development of Judaism.
sources: Robert T. Anderson and Terry Giles, 'The Samaritan Pentateuch: An Introduction to Its Origin, History, and Significance for Biblical Studies' (2012)
